arXiv:2504.17965v1  [quant-ph]  24 Apr 2025
Quantum Fisher-Yates shuﬄe: Unifying methods for generati ng uniform
superpositions of permutations
Lennart Binkowski 1, ∗ and Marvin Schwiering 1
1Institut f¨ ur Theoretische Physik, Leibniz Universit¨ at H annover
Uniform superpositions over permutations play a central ro le in quantum error correction, cryp-
tography, and combinatorial optimisation. We introduce a s imple yet powerful quantisation of the
classical Fisher–Yates shuﬄe, yielding a suite of eﬃcient q uantum algorithms for preparing such
superpositions on composite registers. Our method replace s classical randomness with coherent
control, enabling ﬁve variants that diﬀer in their output st ructure and entanglement with ancillary
systems. We demonstrate that this construction achieves th e best known combination of asymptotic
resources among all existing approaches, requiring only O(n log(n)) qubits and O(n2 log(n)) gates
and circuit depth. These results position the quantum Fishe r–Yates shuﬄe as a strong candidate
for optimality within this class of algorithms. Our work uni ﬁes several prior constructions under
a single, transparent framework and opens up new directions for quantum state preparation using
classical combinatorial insights. Our implementation in Q iskit is available as open-source code,
supporting reproducibility and future exploration of quan tum permutation-based algorithms.
I. INTRODUCTION
Uniform superpositions of permutations have emerged
as a powerful and recurring motif across multiple do-
mains of quantum computing, underpinning advances in
quantum error correction, quantum cryptography, and
quantum-enhanced optimisation. Their utility arises
from the fundamental role that permutation symmetry
plays in encoding, security, and search processes on quan-
tum devices.
In quantum error correction, permutation-invariant
codes exploit the symmetry under qubit permutations
to protect against collective noise, spontaneous decay,
and qubit loss [
1–5]. These codes enable robust encod-
ing schemes that remain invariant under subsystem ex-
change, facilitating resilience in noisy intermediate-scale
quantum (NISQ) environments.
In quantum cryptography, superpositions over permu-
tations enable provably secure encryption protocols, such
as the quantum permutation pad [
6], and support key-
less secure communication [ 7]. These structures also play
an increasingly prominent role in post-quantum cryp-
tography [
8–10], where they serve as building blocks
for quantum-secure primitives and as analytical tools in
oracle-based lower bounds.
In quantum optimisation, many combinatorial prob-
lems—most notably the Travelling Salesperson Prob-
lem (TSP)—are naturally encoded as search problems
over the space of permutations. Uniform superpositions
of permutations are thus integral to quantum heuris-
tic methods such as Grover-based search [
11–15] and
QAOA [ 16–19], where they facilitate exploration of the
feasible solution space. These approaches extend nat-
urally to more complex scheduling and routing prob-
lems [
20–26], where permutations deﬁne the structure of
feasible assignments.
∗ lennart.binkowski@itp.uni-hannover.de
Beyond these applications, uniform superpositions
over permutations also appear in theoretical contexts
such as quantum complexity theory [
27] and entangle-
ment theory [ 28], further underscoring their foundational
character.
The ﬁrst known quantum circuit for generating a uni-
form superposition over all permutations was proposed
by Barenco et al. [
1], employing monotone factorisation
to decompose permutations into a sequence of transpo-
sitions, each conditioned via ancilla registers prepared in
uniform superpositions. Their circuit exempliﬁes early
instances of the now-standard Linear Combination of
Unitaries (LCU) framework [
29], with each controlled
SWAP-gate implementing a unitary in the sum.
Subsequent constructions have explored alternative
permutation encodings. Chiew et al. [30] employed mixed
radix representations [ 31], encoding permutations into
integer vectors and reconstructing them via sequences
of adjacent transpositions. Similar encodings, includ-
ing Lehmer codes [
32], were used in [ 33]. Meanwhile,
B¨ artschi and Eidenbenz [18] proposed a method for gen-
erating uniform superpositions of n ×n permutation ma-
trices by iteratively building row vectors under column
constraints, using ancilla registers to enforce orthogonal-
ity conditions in superposition.
More recently, Majenz et al. [
10] revived the mono-
tone factorisation perspective to formalise permuta-
tion oracles, while Adhikari [
34] adapted the classical
Steinhaus–Johnson–Trotter algorithm [ 35–37] for ran-
dom sampling in quantum circuits, reversing the direc-
tion of adjacent swaps used in earlier works. Bai and
Shang [
15] proposed an iterative insertion-based strategy
which also relies on the monotone factorisation.
Despite the proliferation of constructions, many recent
approaches rediscover a small set of core techniques, of-
ten rooted in classical combinatorics. This redundancy
suggests a need for conceptual uniﬁcation and principled
design strategies. In this work, we address this gap by
presenting a streamlined derivation of quantum circuits
2
Algorithm 1: Fisher Yates(a)
1 for i← n− 1 to 1 do
2 j← random integer such that 0 ≤ j≤ i;
3 exchange a[j] and a[i];
4 end
Algorithm 2: Reversed Fisher Yates(a)
1 for i← 1 to n− 1 do
2 j← random integer such that 0 ≤ j≤ i;
3 exchange a[j] and a[i];
4 end
FIG. 1. The Fisher-Yates shuﬄe as proposed by Durstenfeld [ 38] and it’s reversed version.
based on the classical Fisher-Yates shuﬄe [ 39]. This ap-
proach not only recovers the original circuit of Barenco
et al. [
40] as a special case, but also provides a clear
path from classical random sampling to coherent quan-
tum state preparation.
By leveraging the Fisher–Yates algorithm and coupling
it with eﬃcient quantum subroutines for uniform super-
position preparation [
41], we construct quantum circuits
that are asymptotically optimal in terms of qubit count,
gate complexity, and depth among existing algorithms.
We provide a publicly available Qiskit implementation of
these circuits is available under [
42]. In doing so, we aim
to provide a unifying and practically eﬃcient framework
for permutation-based quantum algorithms across a wide
range of applications.
II. THE OBJECTIVE
Let us ﬁrst precisely state the objective we wish to
tackle with our circuit construction. For a given number
n ∈ ℕ, we consider n multi-qubit registers, each com-
prising ⌈log2(n)⌉ qubits. A single permutation σ ∈ Sn,
expressed in inverted “one-line notation”/“word repre-
sentation” σ −1(0)σ −1(1) · · ·σ −1(n − 1), is represented
by the product state

III. QUANTUM FISHER-YATES SHUFFLE
The classical Fisher-Yates shuﬄe [ 39], assuming access
to a true random number generator, shuﬄes an input ar-
ray of length n with a random permutation uniformly
drawn from Sn. By simply inputting an array with
a[k] = k, i.e. the identity’s (inverted) word representa-
tion, we may therefore use the algorithm to directly gen-
erate the inverted word representation of a random per-
mutation of n elements drawn uniformly from Sn. The
original shuﬄe operates in time O(n2). Subsequently, the
algorithm has been improved by Durstenfeld [
38] to op-
erate in time O(n), which clearly constitutes the optimal
runtime attainable for this task involving acting on n el-
ements. Durstenfeld’s version simply iterates through all
array indices i except the ﬁrst one in descending order,
samples for each i an exchange index j uniformly at ran-
dom between 0 and i, and exchanges the array elements
at position i andj (see
Algorithm 1 for the pseudo code).
An equally valid approach is to iterate through the ar-
ray index i in ascending order, but to leave the rest of
the algorithm at it is (see Algorithm 2 for corresponding
pseudo code). We use this reversed version as the basis
for a quantised Fisher-Yates shuﬄe as it has the con-
ceptual advantage that when terminating the loop pre-
maturely, say after iteration i, the ﬁrst i + 1 elements
are permuted randomly with a permutation uniformly
drawn from Si+1
2 It is fundamentally based on the ob-
servation that when building all the compositions of an
elementσ ∈Si with one of the transpositions τj,i := (ji ),
2 The reversed Fisher-Yates shuﬄe with it’s recursive struct ure is
conceptually similar to the Steinhaus-Johnson-Trotter al gorithm
used for successive generation of all n-element permutations.
0 ≤ j ≤ i, which exchange the numbers j and i, one
obtains the entire Si+1. Note that this an equivalent,
recursive reformulation of the uniqueness of the mono-
tone factorisation and also formalised in the subsequent
lemma.
Lemma 1. Let i ∈ ℕ. For a permutation σ ∈ Si, let
˜σ ∈Si+1 be deﬁned via ˜σ (k) :=σ (k) for 0 ≤k ≤i−1 and
˜σ (i) :=i. Then there is a bijection between {0,...,i }×Si
and Si+1 via (j,σ ) ↦→τj,i ˜σ .
Proof. Since both sets have a cardinality of ( i + 1)!, it
suﬃces to establish injectivity of the proposed mapping.
To this end, let ( j,σ ), (k,π ) ∈ { 0,...,i } ×Si such that
τj,i ˜σ = τk,i ˜π . Since both ˜ σ and ˜π act trivially on the
elementi, we immediately obtain

Multiplying both sides of τj,i ˜σ =τj,i ˜π with τj,i from the
left then establishes ˜σ = ˜π , i.e. σ =π .
We quantise Algorithm 2 in a straight-forward way by
keeping the overall loop, but replacing lines 2 and 3 with
creating a uniform superposition of all states |j⟩a with
0 ≤ j ≤ i in the ancilla register and subsequently con-
trolling each of the swaps of the j-th and i-th subregister
of the permutation register (and the data register in case
of shuﬄing) on the ancilla register being in the state |j⟩a,
respectively. Prior to this, we initialise the permutation
register in the identity’s (inverted) word representation
to generate the desired uniform superposition of all per-
mutations’ inverted word representations.

IV. RESOURCE ANALYSIS
Throughout this section, we assume m ∈ ℕ and n ∈ ℕ
to be ﬁxed, but arbitrary. For a given number k ∈ ℕ,
∆( k) denotes it’s Hamming weight, i.e. the number of
ones in it’s binary representation. Furthermore, we de-
note with Q(W ) the number of qubits on which a unitary
W acts non-trivially, with G(W ) the gate count of W ,
and with C(W ) it’s circuit depth/cycle count.
1. Number of qubits
First, the disentangling state preparation (
Algo-
rithm 3 ) acts on an n⌈log2(n)⌉-qubit permutation reg-
ister Hp and uses an ancilla register Ha for applying the
swaps of subregisters of Hp in superposition. Since the
ancilla register’s state is uncomputed after each itera-
tion (lines 10-12), we can reuse it’s qubits for the sub-
sequent one. Therefore, it’s size is determined by how
many qubits we need at most at once to represent any
of the computational basis states on which we control
the subregister swaps. This maximum is readily attained
in the last iteration where we have to store the num-
ber n − 1 in binary, yielding the required register size of
|n − 1|bin = ⌈log2(n)⌉. In summary, we obtain
Q(A) = n⌈log2(n)⌉ + ⌈log2(n)⌉ = (n + 1)⌈log2(n)⌉. (7)
Second, the entangling state preparation (
Algorithm 4)
also operates on an n⌈log2(n)⌉-qubit permutation regis-
ter Hp, but requires a larger ancilla register Ha since it
does not uncompute the ancilla register after an itera-
tion. Therefore, the i-th iteration has to be carried out
on a fresh set of qubits. The largest number we have to
represent during iteration i is indeed the number i; the
corresponding subregister of Ha has to contain at least
|i|bin qubits. In summary, this yields a qubit count of
Q( ˜A) = n⌈log2(n)⌉ +
n−1∑

|i|bin. (8)

Algorithm 5 ) requires
the same registers as the disentangling state preparation
plus an additional data register to be shuﬄed. Assuming
n data points and m qubits per data point, this yields a
total qubit count of
Q(B) = Q(A) +mn = (n + 1)⌈log2(n)⌉ +mn. (9)

Al-

2. Gate count
Next, we consider the gate counts for the ﬁve algo-
rithms. The initialisation of the permutation register,
which is part of the ﬁrst four methods, ﬂips the qubits
of the k-th subregister Hp:k to yield the binary repre-
sentation of the number k, where 1 ≤ k ≤ n − 1. The
number of X-gates necessary to obtain |k⟩p:k from |0⟩p:k
is simply the Hamming weight of k, ∆( k), i.e. the num-
ber of ones in k’s binary representation. In each iteration
1 ≤i ≤n − 1, we have to account for the cost of imple-
menting the uniform state preparation Ui of the ﬁrst i+1
computational basis states as well as of the implemen-
tation costs of the i multi-controlled subregister swaps.
6
A full (multi-controlled) subregister swap is comprised
of ⌈log2(n)⌉ individual (multi-controlled) SWAP-gates. A
SWAP-gate, in turn, can be decomposed into three CX-
gates with alternating control and target qubit. If the
SWAP-gate is controlled, it suﬃces to merely control the
middle CX-gate on the same qubits (compare [
43, Exercise
4.25]). Lastly, in order to disentangle the ancilla register
Ha after each iteration i, we apply, for all 1 ≤ j ≤ i,
a cascade of ∆( j) X-gates transforming |j⟩a to |0⟩a, but
controlled on the subregister Hp:j being in the state |i⟩p:i
which requires us to control each of the X-gates on |i|bin
qubits. In summary, we obtain a gate count of

3. Circuit depth
Lastly, we consider the cycle counts of the ﬁve algo-
rithms. We make the following assumption which, how-
ever, only plays a role for the initialisation of the permu-
tation register Hp in the ﬁrst four methods: Gates acting
on disjoint qubits can be executed in parallel, constitut-
ing a single quantum cycle. This assumption reduces
the cycle count for the initialisation subroutine to the
cycle count of a single X-gate since all X-gates act on dis-
tinct qubits of the permutation register. For the remain-
ing steps in
Algorithm 3 , we only give an upper bound
on the cycle count in order to avoid distinguishing too
many diﬀerent cases (further parallelisation techniques
are subject to an actual compiler/transpiler): We simply
assume that no gates of two iterations 1 ≤i1,i 2 ≤n − 1
can be applied in parallel, hence the total cycle count is
the sum of each iteration’s cycle count. Furthermore, we
assume that there is no gate overlap between diﬀerent
subroutines within a single iteration. Eﬀectively, we may
therefore replace all subroutines’ gate counts in (
12) with
their respective cycle counts (except for the initialisation
of the permutation register), yielding

4. Analysis of subroutines and individual gates
Let us now inspect the gate and cycle count of the
superposition creating circuit Ui as proposed by [ 41].
In the following, let i ∈ ℕ be arbitrary, but ﬁxed. If
i + 1 = 2 r for some r ∈ ℕ, then the generation of
the uniform superposition of all computational basis |j⟩,
0 ≤j ≤i degenerates to the simple application of H-gates
to each of the |i|bin qubits. In this case, we thus obtain
G(Ui) = |i|binG(H) and C(Ui) = C(H). Otherwise, let
s0,...,s ∆( i+1) ∈ ℕ denote the positions of the non-zero
bits of the binary representation of i + 1, where we start
counting from zero and s0 corresponds to the position of
the least signiﬁcant bit. Following the pseudo code in
[
41, Algorithm 1], we can easily infer a gate count of
G(Ui) = s0G(H) + G(RY) + (∆( i + 1) − 1)G(CRY)
+
∆( i+1)∑
k=1
(
sk −sk−1
)
G(CH).
(22)
From all operations within Ui, only the initial layer of
Hadamard gates on all qubits before the least signiﬁcant
qubit may be applied in parallel, that is
C(Ui) = min(1,s 0)C(H)+C(RY)+(∆( i + 1) − 1)×
C(CRY) +
∆( i+1)∑
k=1

)
C(CH).
(23)
This concludes the gate and cycle count for all “high-
level” subroutines. We expressed all their resource re-
quirements in terms of implementation costs of X-gates,
H-gates, and RY-gates, their respective singly-controlled
versions as well as multiply-controlled X-gates. The
single-qubit gates X, H, and RY are typically native gates
or can be synthesised from other native gates with small
overhead [
44–46]. In practice, this means that 1 ≤
G(X), G(H), G(RY) ≤ 4 (the cycle counts are identical
to the gate counts) [ 44–46]. Their controlled versions
(with the partial exception of the CX-gate) are usually
not naively supported on any architecture and have to
be synthesised from the available architecture-speciﬁc
two-qubit gate and additional single-qubit gates. The
following bounds are realistic for non-photonic architec-
tures: 1 ≤ G(CX), G(CH), G(CRY) ≤ 39 (again, the cycle
counts match the gate counts) [
44–46]. The use of error-
correcting codes will generally alter the set of natively
available logical gates, with the resulting set being highly
dependent on the hardware and code employed [
47]. In
contrast, some error-mitigation techniques allow for util-
ising the full breadth of native physical gates [
48–50].
Lastly, determining the gate and cycle count for CmX-
gates, m ≥ 2, is more nuanced than the previous consid-
erations. In the special case m = 2, i.e. the case of the
Toﬀoli gate, the usual ancilla-free (and also optimal [ 51])
decomposition requires six CX-gates and nine single-qubit
gates (see e.g. [ 43, Figure 4.9]). Without any additional
ancilla qubits, the best known decompositions of a CmX-
gate requires O(2m) CX-gates and single-qubit gates [ 40,
Lemma 7.1]. This can be drastically reduced once suﬃ-
ciently many ancilla qubits are available: For m ≥ 3, a
CmX-gate can be decomposed into 2( m − 1) Toﬀoli gates,
utilisingm− 2 ancilla qubits (see e.g. [
43, Figure 4.10] for
m = 5). This construction assumes each of the ancilla
qubits to be initialised in the |0⟩ state, but also uncom-
putes the ancilla qubits. In our case, however, we do not
want to include more qubits than previously determined
in (
7)–(11). Alternatively, we may always borrow qubits
from a data/permutation subregister which is currently
not swapped or does not control the uncomputation step
for the disentangling versions. Since these qubits are not
guaranteed to be in the |0⟩ state, we have to enlarge
the decomposition to include 4( m − 2) Toﬀoli gates for a
toggle-based implementation and its uncomputation. To
each of these Toﬀoli gates we may now apply the previ-
ously discussed decomposition. In summary, this yields
G(CmX) = 4(m − 2)(6G(CX) + 7G(RZ) + 2G(H)) and the
corresponding cycle count C(CmX) is typically not sub-
stantially smaller.
8
5. Asymptotic resource scaling
From the previous quantitative resource analysis, we
now infer the qualitative asymptotic scaling. First, the
scalings of the respective qubit counts are rather straight-
forward. We readily infer that Q(A) ∈ O (n log(n)) and
that Q(B) ∈ O ((m + log(n))n). For the other three al-
gorithms, we ﬁrst observe that for the ancilla register’s
size it holds that

i=1
|i|bin =
n−1∑
i=1
(⌊log2(i)⌋ + 1) ≤n +
n−1∑
i=1
log2(i)
≤n + log2
( n∏
i=1
i
)
=n + log2(n!) ∈ O(n log(n))
by Stirling’s approximation.
4 Thus, we conclude that
Q( ˜A) ∈ O (n log(n)), Q( ˜B) ∈ O ((m + log(n))n), and
Q(C) ∈ O ((m + log(n))n). Lastly, since the Shukla-
Vedula construction for Ui does not require any ancilla
qubits, it holds that Q(Ui) ∈ O(log(i)).
We continue with the asymptotic scaling of gate and
cycle count. As it turns out, both quantities scale iden-
tically for all presented algorithms; therefore, it is suf-
ﬁcient to focus on the gate count in the following. On
the most elementary level, we state that X-gate, H-gate,
RY-gate, and RZ-gate as well as their singly-controlled
versions can be decomposed into a constant number of
native gates. This also implies that G(CmX) ∈ O (m).
Furthermore, a number’s Hamming weight is trivially
upper bounded by the number’s binary length, hence
∆( i) ∈ O (log(i)). The gate counts for A and ˜A are
(asymptotically) clearly dominated by the costs of im-
plementing i permutation subregister swaps in each it-
eration 1 ≤ i ≤ n − 1, scaling as O(i log(n)2). Exe-
cuting the summation over all iterations establishes that
G(A), G( ˜A) ∈ O(n2 log(n)2). Similarly, for B and ˜B, the
most costly components are the i data and permutation
register swaps per iteration, pushing their gate counts
to lie in O((m + log(n))n2 log(n)). With an analogous
reasoning we also obtain that G(C) ∈ O (mn2 log(n)).
Moreover, as already pointed out by Shukla and Vedula
[
41], the gate count for their state preparation circuit
Ui scales as O(log(i)). The qubit and gate/cycle count
complexities are again summarised in Table I.
There is a generally applicable trade-oﬀ between the
size of the ancilla register and the gate and cycle count
of each of our methods: Instead of using a ⌈log2(n)⌉-qubit
ancilla register for A andB, we may introduce n − 1 an-
cilla qubits and prepare, in each iteration 1 ≤i ≤n − 1,

n log(n) ∈ O (Q( ˜A)). Following the subsequent derivations thor-

putational basis states with Hamming weight one. The
respective state preparation circuit Vi is sketched in [
1]
and requires O(i) gates and cycles. This one-hot en-
coding of the control structures has the advantage that
each of the SWAP-gates only has to be controlled on a sin-
gle qubit, avoiding the decomposition of multi-controlled
gates with an O(log(i)) overhead. The uncomputation
of the ancilla qubits also becomes faster since, for each
1 ≤ j ≤ i, only one multi-controlled X-gate (instead of
∆( j) multi-controlled X-gates) has to be applied to the
ancilla register. Overall, this technique eliminates one
log(n) factor from the asymptotic scaling of gate and
cycle count while introducing O(n) ancilla qubits. At
least for A and B, the additional qubit requirements are
still dominated by the O(n log(n)) qubit demand of the
permutation register. Applying the same technique to
the entangling versions ˜A, ˜B, and C also eliminates one
log(n) factor from the gate/cycle demands, but increases
the asymptotic qubit count: In order to employ the state
preparation Vi, each of the ancilla subregisters Ha:i has
to contain i (instead of |i|bin) qubits, yielding an overall
requirement of O(n2) ancilla qubits. The resulting qubit,
gate, and cycle complexities are summarised in
Table II.
Finally, we compare the just derived qualitative qubit,
gate, and cycle requirements with the ones of similar al-
gorithms found in the literature. Barenco et al. ’s pro-
jection into the symmetrised subspace is almost iden-
tical to Coh, the entangling light shuﬄe with one-hot-
encoded control, and indeed admits the same qubit count
of O((m +n)n) and gate/cycle count of O(mn2). In con-
trast, Chiew et al.’s implementation as well as Adhikary’s
sampling algorithm are based on controlled sequences of
adjacency transpositions and a binary encoding of the
controls. They are conceptually close to our algorithms
˜A and C, the entangling state preparation and the en-
tangling light shuﬄe, with the exception that in each
iteration 1 ≤ i ≤ n − 1, they apply O(i2) controlled
subregister swaps (instead of O(i) controlled subregister
swaps). This places their respective qubit requirements
in O(n log(n)) and O((m+log(n))n), and their respective
gate/cycle counts in O(n3 log(n)2) and O(mn3 log(n)).
5
The method proposed by Bai and Shang [ 15] is concep-
tually close to our algorithm A, the disentangling state
preparation. Correspondingly, they also achieve a qubit
requirement of O(n log(n)). However, their gate and cy-
cle requirements are of order O(n5/ 2 log(n)) and thus
higher than for our SWAP-based approach.

arise as a special case of any of our algorithms. This is
due to the fact that they represent the permutations of
n elements as n ×n permutation matrices whose entries
correspond to single qubits. In this lighter encoding of
permutations (n2 instead of n⌈log2(n)⌉ qubits), they can
entirely avoid an ancilla register during the state prepa-
ration and have “enough space” to parallelise several con-
trolled SWAP-gates in each iteration. In summary, they re-
quire O(n2) qubits, O(n3) gates, and O(n2) cycles. That
is, at the expense of a larger qubit demand, they are able
to beat algorithm Aoh in terms of cycle requirements, but
also require more (unparallelised) gates.
V. CONCLUSION
In this work, we presented a natural and eﬃcient quan-
tisation of the classical Fisher–Yates shuﬄe, leading to a
family of quantum algorithms for preparing uniform su-
perpositions over permutations of composite qubit reg-
isters. Our quantisation principle is straightforward:
whenever a classical operation Dj depends on a uniformly
sampled integer j ∈ J, we replace it with a uniform su-
perposition over all j ∈ J encoded in an ancilla register
and apply the quantised version of Dj, controlled on the
ancilla being in state |j⟩.
We proposed ﬁve algorithmic variants tailored to diﬀer-
ent objectives: constructing uniform superpositions over
the inverted word representations of all permutations (ei-
ther entangled with an ancilla or disentangled), generat-
ing superpositions of shuﬄed composite registers with the
corresponding inverted word representations stored in an
auxiliary register (again, with or without entanglement),
and preparing uniform superpositions of shuﬄed registers
entangled with an ancilla. To support reproducibility and
facilitate further research, we provide an open-source im-
plementation of all algorithms using Qiskit.
Our resource analysis demonstrates that the quantum
Fisher–Yates shuﬄe achieves an asymptotic optimum
among known constructions targeting this problem class.
Speciﬁcally, it operates with only O(n log(n)) qubits and
achieves a gate and cycle complexity of O(n2 log(n)).
In contrast, alternative approaches either require more
qubits – e.g., the method in [
18] has a depth of only
O(n2), but needs O(n2) qubits and O(n3) gates – or in-
cur higher gate and depth complexity when constrained
to O(n log(n)) qubits, as in [
30] and [ 15].
These observations lead us to conjecture that the quan-
tum Fisher–Yates shuﬄe may be optimal within a formal
framework that remains to be precisely deﬁned. This
intuition is bolstered by the classical Fisher–Yates shuf-
ﬂe’s optimal linear runtime for sampling uniform random
permutations. We strongly suspect that any alternative
quantisation of a classically suboptimal method will not
surpass the quantum Fisher–Yates construction. Nev-
ertheless, it remains possible that intrinsically quantum
strategies, without classical analogues, might yield supe-
rior performance. We leave this as an open and intriguing
direction for future research.
A natural extension of our work involves generalising
the data register transformations beyond simple subreg-
ister swaps. One could imagine using arbitrary unitary
representations of the symmetric group acting on some
Hilbert space Hd, controlled by an ancilla Ha that records
the applied permutation in superposition. While such a
generalisation is straightforward in the entangled case,
constructing disentangled versions is more subtle: these
require the ancilla to be uncomputed after each iteration
1 ≤ i ≤ n − 1, where transpositions τj,i , 0 ≤ j < i,
are applied in superposition. If the action of a transposi-
tion cannot be unambiguously inferred from the resulting
state of Hd, uncomputation becomes impossible. Deter-
mining which representations of the symmetric group ad-
mit this iterative uncomputation remains an open prob-
lem for future investigation.
